name: erdos_mollin_walsh
claim_latex: There do not exist three consecutive positive integers $(n, n+1, n+2)$ all of which are powerful (a positive integer $m$ is powerful iff every prime $p \mid m$ satisfies $p^2 \mid m$).

given_facts.statement: For all $n \leq 10^{14}$, no triple $(n, n+1, n+2)$ has all three powerful.
given_facts.sign_disambiguation: POSITIVE existence claim about the *non-existence* of triples up to a finite bound. The bound is an UPPER bound on $n$ (the smallest possibly-powerful triple, if any exists, has $n > 10^{14}$). Treating this as a proof of the conjecture is a sign error: it only rules out small witnesses.
given_facts.citation: stated as F1 in the project prompt; matches Mollin–Walsh-style computational searches
warns: F1 only rules out small $n$. A witness must have $n > 10^{14}$. Any chain that concludes 'EMW is proved' from F1 alone is BLOCKING.

given_facts.statement: Granville–Mollin–Walsh: the abc conjecture implies that the set of $n$ with $(n, n+1, n+2)$ all powerful is *finite*.
given_facts.sign_disambiguation: FINITENESS, not non-existence. Even under abc, finitely many counterexamples may exist. F2 does NOT imply EMW; it conditionally implies a quantitative version (an explicit upper bound on $n$).
warns: Reading F2 as 'abc implies EMW' is a sign error: abc gives finiteness but not zero counterexamples.

given_facts.statement: If $(n, n+1, n+2)$ are all powerful with $n+1 = a^2 b$ ($b$ squarefree), then the gap structure forces $a, b$ to satisfy a Pell-type equation $X^2 - DY^2 = \pm 1$ for $D$ depending on the squarefree parts of $n, n+1, n+2$. The number of solutions to such Pell equations is finite for each fixed $D$.
given_facts.sign_disambiguation: FINITELY MANY per Pell instance, but $D$ ranges over infinitely many squarefree triples. F3 does NOT imply finiteness of the union over all $D$. Concluding 'finitely many EMW triples' from F3 alone is a sign error.
warns: F3 controls Pell solutions for fixed squarefree parts. The aggregate over varying $D$ is unbounded; F3 alone is not enough to close EMW.

witness_schema.n: int >= 2 — the candidate smallest element; verifier checks n, n+1, n+2 are all powerful
witness_schema.claimed_smallest: int (optional) — agent's claim for the smallest powerful triple; verifier recomputes

note: Erdős–Mollin–Walsh conjecture. Two-track problem: Track 1 search loop hunts for a witness $n$ via sieving; Track 2 proof loop attempts a proof under the F1+F2+F3 ledger. Witness is a single integer $n$ with $n, n+1, n+2$ all powerful — verifier factors all three by trial division up to sqrt(max), checks every prime factor has multiplicity >= 2. Score = the smallest such $n$ (chosen so larger witnesses don't strictly outrank smaller ones — a single witness suffices). Computer-verified up to ~10^14 via Heath-Brown / Mollin–Walsh; any witness has $n > 10^{14}$, so the verifier must accept arbitrary-precision integers.
